aiPersona:
You are an expert irrigation contractor assistant with deep knowledge of residential and commercial landscape irrigation systems.
You understand the full range of sprinkler head types including rotors, spray heads, drip emitters, micro-sprayers, and bubblers.
You know that rotor heads are ideal for large turf areas (25-50 ft radius) while spray heads cover smaller zones (5-15 ft radius).
You are familiar with drip irrigation for planting beds, gardens, and trees, and you know that drip reduces water waste significantly.
You understand zone design principles — grouping heads by precipitation rate, sun exposure, plant type, and soil conditions.
You know standard pipe sizes (3/4 inch, 1 inch, 1-1/4 inch mainline) and that PVC is standard in warm climates while poly pipe is used in freeze areas.
You are experienced with irrigation controllers including traditional timer-based models and smart Wi-Fi controllers with weather-based adjustments.
You understand that smart controllers can reduce water use by 20-40 percent through evapotranspiration-based scheduling.
You know that proper system design starts with measuring water pressure and flow rate at the meter to size zones correctly.
You understand backflow prevention requirements — RPZ valves, double-check valves, and atmospheric vacuum breakers per local code.
You are familiar with valve boxes, solenoid valves, and wiring standards for multi-zone systems.
You know that winterization (blowing out lines with compressed air) is essential in freeze climates to prevent pipe and head damage.
You understand spring startup procedures including opening valves, checking for breaks, adjusting heads, and programming the controller.
You know that head-to-head coverage is the standard for uniform water distribution.
You factor in trenching costs for new installations and the option of pulling pipe versus open-trench methods.
You are familiar with water audits and can help optimize existing systems for water conservation.
You communicate clearly about water schedules, run times, and watering restrictions that vary by municipality.

defaultLineItems.description: Wi-Fi smart controller with weather-based scheduling, 8-12 zones
Gear-driven rotor heads with adjustable arc, 25-45 ft radius
Pop-up spray heads with matched precipitation nozzles, 4-15 ft radius
Drip tubing, emitters, filters, and pressure regulators for bed zones
Electric solenoid valves for each irrigation zone

defaultLineItems.itemName: PVC Pipe and Fittings (mainline and lateral)
defaultLineItems.description: Schedule 40 PVC mainline and Class 200 lateral pipe with fittings
Standard and jumbo valve boxes with lids for underground access
RPZ or double-check backflow preventer per local code
Multi-strand irrigation wire and waterproof connectors for valve wiring
Trench, lay pipe, install heads, wire valves, and backfill
Program controller zones, set run times, adjust all heads for coverage
Inspect system, identify broken or malfunctioning heads and lines
Replace broken or clogged pop-up spray heads with matching models
Replace broken gear-driven rotors with matching models
Replace worn or clogged nozzles for proper spray pattern
Dig up, replace, and adjust sprinkler heads
Locate and repair broken PVC pipe section
Upgrade to smart controller with app control and weather-based scheduling
Wireless rain and freeze sensor to prevent watering during rain or freeze events
Inline flow meter for leak detection and water usage monitoring
Remove old controller, mount new unit, wire all zones and test
Run each zone, check coverage, adjust run times for new smart controller
Blow out all zones with high-volume compressor to remove water from lines and heads

defaultLineItems.itemName: Shut Off Water Supply and Drain
defaultLineItems.description: Turn off water supply, drain backflow preventer, and open drain valves
Set controller to rain mode or off, note zone settings for spring restart
Insulate exposed backflow preventer against freeze damage
Pre-book spring startup at discounted rate when booking winterization
Slowly open main water supply and pressurize system
Activate each zone, check for breaks, leaks, and head damage from winter
Adjust head height, arc, and radius. Clean clogged nozzles and filters.
Set watering schedule based on current conditions and local restrictions
Test backflow device and certify as required by local water authority
Replacement heads, nozzles, or fittings for any damage found during startup

terminologyGlossary.rotor: A gear-driven sprinkler head that rotates a single stream across a large area, 25-50 ft radius
terminologyGlossary.spray head: A pop-up sprinkler that fans water in a fixed pattern over a smaller area, 5-15 ft radius
terminologyGlossary.drip emitter: A low-volume irrigation device that delivers water directly to plant root zones
terminologyGlossary.zone: A group of sprinkler heads controlled by one valve, designed for similar water needs
terminologyGlossary.precipitation rate: The rate at which water is applied to an area, measured in inches per hour
terminologyGlossary.head-to-head coverage: Design standard where each head throws water to the adjacent head for uniform coverage
terminologyGlossary.backflow preventer: A device that prevents irrigation water from flowing back into the potable water supply
terminologyGlossary.RPZ valve: Reduced Pressure Zone valve - the most reliable type of backflow preventer
terminologyGlossary.solenoid valve: An electrically operated valve that opens and closes to control water flow to each zone
terminologyGlossary.mainline: The pressurized pipe from the water source to the zone valves
terminologyGlossary.lateral line: The pipe from a zone valve to the sprinkler heads it controls
terminologyGlossary.GPM: Gallons per minute - the flow rate measurement used to size zones and pipes
terminologyGlossary.PSI: Pounds per square inch - the water pressure measurement critical for head selection
terminologyGlossary.ET-based scheduling: Evapotranspiration-based watering that adjusts run times based on weather and plant needs
terminologyGlossary.winterization: The process of removing water from irrigation pipes using compressed air before freezing weather

seasonalTips.tip: Spring startup season is here. Book early as every customer wants service in the same 2-week window. Upsell controller upgrades.
Peak watering season. Market system audits and nozzle upgrades for water conservation. Smart controller upgrades save customers money.
Adjust schedules for fall watering. Begin booking winterization appointments before the rush. Check for leaks before shutdown.
Winterization crunch time. Ensure compressor is serviced and ready. Pre-book spring startups at a discount to lock in revenue.

followUpToneNotes: Irrigation customers appreciate water savings and convenience. Emphasize smart controller benefits and water conservation. Be practical about scheduling around weather and municipal watering restrictions. Winterization and spring startup are excellent recurring service opportunities. Help customers understand that a well-maintained system saves water and money long-term.
